questions.question: Define the Cut Property used in Prim's Algorithm and explain why it guarantees the greedy choice is safe.
keyPoints: Cut: partition of vertices into two non-empty sets S and V-S
Minimum weight edge crossing any cut belongs to some MST
This guarantees adding the minimum edge crossing the current cut never leads to suboptimal solution
Proof by contradiction: assuming optimal MST doesn't contain minimum crossing edge leads to cycle

questions.question: Compare Prim's Algorithm and Kruskal's Algorithm in terms of their approach, data structures used, and time complexities.
keyPoints: Prim grows single tree from starting vertex; Kruskal grows forest merging edges
Prim uses priority queue of vertices with key values; Kruskal uses priority queue of edges
Prim: O(V²) with arrays, O(E log V) with binary heap, O(E + V log V) with Fibonacci heap
Kruskal: O(E log V) with union-find, works well on sparse graphs

questions.question: Explain why Prim's Algorithm is considered a greedy algorithm. What is its greedy choice property?
keyPoints: Greedy: makes locally optimal choice at each step hoping for global optimum
Greedy choice: always pick minimum weight edge connecting MST to a vertex outside
Choice is safe due to cut property - minimum edge crossing the cut is in some MST
No backtracking or reconsideration of decisions once made

questions.question: For a graph with n vertices, how many edges does any spanning tree contain? Prove that any MST must contain exactly n-1 edges.
keyPoints: A spanning tree is a connected acyclic subgraph with all n vertices
For n vertices, any tree must have exactly n-1 edges (proved by induction or cycle argument
Adding any edge to a tree creates exactly one cycle; removing one edge breaks the cycle
MST is a spanning tree with minimum weight, so it must have n-1 edges

questions.question: Analyze the time complexity of Prim's Algorithm using (i) Array implementation and (ii) Binary Heap implementation. When is each preferred?
keyPoints: Array: O(V²) - scan all vertices to find min key, update all neighbors - simpler, no heap overhead
Binary Heap: O(E log V) - extract-min and decrease-key both take O(log V)
Array preferred for dense graphs (E ≈ V²), Heap preferred for sparse graphs (E ≈ V)
Fibonacci heap gives O(E + V log V) but constants are high

questions.question: Does Prim's Algorithm work correctly for graphs with negative edge weights? Justify your answer.
keyPoints: Yes, Prim's Algorithm works for any real-valued edge weights including negatives
The cut property and greedy choice property hold regardless of sign of edge weights
Negative cycles would be problematic for shortest path but MSTs have no cycles by definition
Only requirement is graph be connected and undirected with numeric edge weights

questions.question: What is a Minimum Spanning Forest? Under what condition does Prim's Algorithm produce a forest instead of a single MST?
keyPoints: Minimum Spanning Forest: collection of MSTs, one for each connected component
Prim's produces a forest when the input graph is disconnected
Algorithm can be run by starting from any vertex, building MST for that component, then processing remaining components
Total weight is sum of weights of individual component MSTs

questions.question: Provide a detailed proof that Prim's Algorithm always produces a Minimum Spanning Tree. Use the cut property in your proof.
keyPoints: Proof by induction on number of edges added: Base case - single vertex has trivial MST
Inductive hypothesis: After k edges, the tree is part of some MST
Inductive step: Let the (k+1)th edge e be minimum crossing current cut (visited vs unvisited)
By cut property, e belongs to some MST; if our tree T isn't that MST, construct T' = T + e - f where f is some edge in that MST
Show T' is also MST with same weight, and now contains e; continue until all vertices included
Conclusion: Final tree is MST with total weight equal to optimal

questions.question: Trace the execution of Prim's Algorithm on the following graph starting from vertex A. Show all steps, the contents of the priority queue, and the final MST edges with total weight. Graph: A-B:7, A-D:5, B-C:8, B-E:7, C-E:5, D-E:15, D-F:6, E-F:8, E-G:9, F-G:11
keyPoints: Initialize: key[A]=0, others=∞, parent[A]=nil
Extract min: Add A to MST, update keys of D(5), B(7)
Extract min: Add D to MST, update E(15), F(6)
Extract min: Add F to MST, update E(8), G(11)
Extract min: Add B to MST, update C(8), E(7) - key[E] decreased from 15 to 7
Extract min: Add E to MST, update C(5), G(9) - key[C] decreased from 8 to 5, G from 11 to 9
Extract min: Add C to MST (via E, weight 5), update nothing new
Extract min: Add G to MST (via E, weight 9)
MST edges: A-D(5), D-F(6), B-E(7), E-C(5), E-G(9) - wait need correct parent tracing...
Final: A→D(5), D→F(6), B→E(7), E→C(5), E→G(9) = total 32. Actually recheck sequence and weights carefully

questions.question: Design and analyze an algorithm to find the second minimum spanning tree (2nd MST) of a graph. Hint: Consider the concept of replacing one edge in the MST.
keyPoints: Second MST differs from MST by at least one edge
Approach: For each edge e in MST, try replacing it with the minimum edge not in MST that connects the two components created by removing e
Find minimum spanning edge for each cut - this is essentially finding the minimum edge that doesn't create a cycle when added but is as expensive as possible while still being minimal
Algorithm: For each edge in MST, find minimum weight edge crossing that cut (not in MST) and compute total
Complexities: O(E × (V-1)) if done naively, better approaches use advanced data structures
Alternative view: second best MST differs by exactly one edge replacement in most cases
